title: Maclaurin series of sin(x), cos(x), and eˣ
description: Use the Maclaurin series of sin(x), cos(x), and eˣ to solve problems about various power series and functions.
topic_path: Finding Taylor or Maclaurin series for a function
Maclaurin series of sin(x), cos(x), and eˣ

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the first three non-zero terms of the Maclaurin polynomial for the function $f(x)={{e}^{2x}}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Start with the Maclaurin series for $e^x$.

$\displaystyle e^x=1\text{ }+\text{ }x\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2!}\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}\text{ }+\ldots +\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{n}}}{n!}\text{ }+\ldots$

Substitute $2x$ for $x$.

$\displaystyle e^{2x}=1\text{ }+\text{ 2}x\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{4{{x}^{2}}}{2!}\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{8{{x}^{3}}}{3!}\text{ }+\ldots +\text{ }\frac{{{(2x)}^{n}}}{n!}\text{ }+\ldots $

Then the first three terms are

$1~+~2x~+~\dfrac{4x^2}{2!}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the first three non-zero terms of the Maclaurin series for the function $f(x)=x\cos(x)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Start with the Maclaurin series for $\cos(x)$.

$\displaystyle\cos x=1-\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2!}+\frac{{{x}^{4}}}{4!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...$

Multiply the series by $x$.

$\displaystyle x\cos x=x-\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{2!}+\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{4!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...$

The first three terms are

$x-\dfrac{{{x}^{3}}}{2!}+\dfrac{{{x}^{5}}}{4!}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the first three non-zero terms of the Maclaurin series for the function $f(x)=\sin(2x)$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Start with the Maclaurin series for $\sin (x)$.

$\displaystyle\sin (x)=x-\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}+\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...$

Substitute $2x$ for $x$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\sin (2x)
\\\\
&=2x-\dfrac{(2x)^3}{3!}+\dfrac{(2x)^5}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\dfrac{{{(2x)}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...
\\\\
&=2x-\dfrac{8x^3}{3!}+\dfrac{32x^5}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\dfrac{{{(2x)}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...
\end{align}$

The first three non-zero terms are

$2x-\dfrac{8x^3}{3!}+\dfrac{32x^5}{5!}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the first three non-zero terms of the Maclaurin polynomial for the function $f(x)=\dfrac{2}{3}{{e}^{3x}}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Start with the Maclaurin series for $e^x$.

$\displaystyle e^x=1\text{ }+\text{ }x\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2!}\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}\text{ }+\ldots +\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{n}}}{n!}\text{ }+\ldots \,$

Substitute $3x$ for $x$.

$\displaystyle e^{3x}=1\text{ }+3x\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{9{{x}^{2}}}{2!}\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{27{{x}^{3}}}{3!}\text{ }+\ldots +\text{ }\frac{{{(3x)}^{n}}}{n!}\text{ }+\ldots$

Multiply by $\dfrac{2}{3}$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\frac{2}{3}e^{3x}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{3}+2x+\dfrac{6{{x}^{2}}}{2!}+\dfrac{18{{x}^{3}}}{3!}+\ldots +\dfrac{2{{(3x)}^{n}}}{3n!}\text{ }+\ldots
\\\\
&=\dfrac{2}{3}+2x+3x^2+3x^3+\ldots+\dfrac{2{{(3x)}^{n}}}{3n!}+\ldots
\end{align}$

The first three non-zero terms are

$\dfrac{2}{3}+2x+3x^2$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What are the first three non-zero terms of the Maclaurin series for $\dfrac{\sin(x)}{x}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Start with the Maclaurin series for $\sin x$.

$\displaystyle\sin x=x-\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}+\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...$

Divide by $x$.

$\displaystyle\frac{\sin x}{x}=1-\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{3!}+\frac{{{x}^{4}}}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...$

The first three terms are

$1~-~\dfrac{x^2}{3!}~+~\dfrac{x^4}{5!}$.

Note that the terms of the given series alternate in sign, have only **odd** factorials in the denominator, and have **odd** powers of $~\pi~$ in the numerator.

This suggests that we work with the Maclaurin series for $~\sin x\,$.

Recall the Maclaurin series for $\sin(x)$.

$\displaystyle\sin x=x-\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}+\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...$

The given series has $\pi$ instead of $x$ throughout. Hence, the given series converges to $\sin(\pi)$.

$\sin(\pi)=0$

Note that the series has terms that alternate in sign, has **odd** factorials in the denominator, and has **odd** powers of $~\dfrac{1}{2}\pi~$ in the numerator.  That suggests that we can use the Maclaurin series for $~\sin x~$ to find the needed value.

Recall that 

$\displaystyle\sin x=x-\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}+\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...$

It follows that

$\displaystyle\sin\left(\frac{1}{2}x\right)=\frac{x}{2} -\frac{\frac{1}{8}{{x}^{3}}}{3!}+\frac{{\frac{1}{32}{x}^{5}}}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{(\frac{1}{2})^{2n+1}{{x }^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...$

Hence, the given series converges to $\sin\big( \frac{\pi}{2} \big)$.

$\sin \big( \frac{\pi}{2}  \big)=1$

Note that the series has terms that alternate in sign, has **even** factorials in the denominator, and has even powers of $~\pi~$ in the numerator. This suggests that we work with the Maclaurin series for $~\cos x\,$.

Recall that

$\displaystyle\cos x=1-\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2!}+\frac{{{x}^{4}}}{4!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...$

It follows that

$\displaystyle3\cos (x^2)=3-\frac{3{{x}^{4}}}{2!}+\frac{3{{x}^{8}}}{4!}-...+{3{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{4n}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...$
Hence, the given series converges to $~3\cos ( \pi^2  )\,$.

Note that the given series has terms that do **not** alternate in sign that have **all** possible factorials in the denominator.

This suggests that we are dealing with something about $~e^x$.

Recall the Maclaurin series for $~e^x$.

$\displaystyle e^x=1\text{ }+\text{ }x\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2!}\text{ }+\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}\text{ }+\ldots +\text{ }\frac{{{x}^{n}}}{n!}\text{ }+\ldots $

Hence, we see that the given series converges to $~e^{\ln 28}$.

$e^{\ln 28}=28$

We can use the Maclaurin series for ${{e}^{x}}$ as a guide.   Recall that 

$\displaystyle{{e}^{x}}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{{{x}^{n}}}{n!}~$.

Now substitute the given value of $x\,$.

$f(\sqrt{\ln(3)})= {{e}^{{{\left(\sqrt{\ln(3)}\right)}^{2}}}} =  {{e}^{\ln 3}}  = 3\,$

Note that the terms have $~n!~$ in the denominator and that they do **not** alternate in sign. That suggests that we use the Maclaurin series for $~{{e}^{x}}~$ as a guide.   Recall that 

$\displaystyle{{e}^{x}}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{{{x}^{n}}}{n!}\,$.

We see that our series is

 $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{{{x}^{n}}}{2n!}\,=\,\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^{n}}{n!}=\,\frac{1}{2}e^x$.

Now substitute $x = \ln(7)$.

$\displaystyle\frac{1}{2}e^x =  \frac{1}{2}e^{\ln(7)}=\frac{7}{2}\,$.

Note that the terms alternate in sign and have **even** factorials in the denominator. This suggests that we can use the Maclaurin series for $\cos x$ as a guide.   Recall that 

$\displaystyle\cos x=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^n\frac{x^{2n}}{(2n)!}\,$.

Hence

$\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^{n}\frac{x^{6n}}{(2n)!}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(-1)^n\frac{(x^3)^{2n}}{(2n)!}=\cos \big(x^3\big)$

Now substitute $~x = \displaystyle\sqrt[3]{\frac{\pi}{2}}\,$.

$\displaystyle\cos \big(x^3\big)= \cos\left(\sqrt[3]{\frac{\pi}{2}}^3\right)=\cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0$

assessment_items.question_content:
**$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{4n+2}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}~~$ is the Maclaurin series for which function?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We need to write out the first several terms of the series in order to see the important properties.

$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{4n+2}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}~=~x^2-\dfrac{x^6}{2!}+\dfrac{x^{10}}{4!}-\dfrac{x^{14}}{6!}+...$

The given series is alternating $\big($because of the $(-1)^n\big)$ and has denominators that are all **even** factorials. This suggests that the series has to do with the function $~\cos x$.

However, we also note that the powers of $x$ go up by **4** in each term $-$ not 2 as would be expected. Thus we see this as a function of $~\cos\big(x^2\big)\,$, not just $~\cos x\,$.  

$\displaystyle \cos~ x=1-\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2!}+\frac{{{x}^{4}}}{4!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{2n}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...$

Hence,

$\displaystyle \cos~ (x^2)=1-\frac{{{(x^2)}^{2}}}{2!}+\frac{{{(x^2)}^{4}}}{4!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{(x^2)}^{2n}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...$

When we multiply through by $x^2\,$, we obtain the following:

$\displaystyle x^2 \cos (x^2)=x^2-\frac{{{x}^{6}}}{2!}+\frac{{{x}^{10}}}{4!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}\frac{{{x}^{4n+2}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...$

assessment_items.question_content:
**$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{-\frac{{{( \frac{2}{3} )}^{n}}{{x}^{n}}}{n!}}~~$ is the Maclaurin series for which function?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Note that the denominators include **all** possible factorials.

This suggests that the given series most closely resembles the Maclaurin series for the function $~{{e}^{x}}$.

However, we have a minus sign in each term, and we have powers of $~\frac{2}{3}x~$ instead of powers of $~x\,$.

Thus we see the following.

$\displaystyle{-e^{\frac{2}{3}x}}=-1-\frac{2}{3}x-\frac{{\frac{4}{9}{x}^{2}}}{2!}-...-\frac{(\frac{2}{3})^n{{x}^{n}}}{n!}+...~=~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{-\frac{{(\frac{2}{3})^{n}}{{x}^{n}}}{n!}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}~~$ is the Maclaurin series for which function?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The given series has terms that alternate in sign and have only **odd** factorials in the denominator.

Thus the given series closely resembles the Maclaurin series for the function $\sin x.$

If we change **every** sign in the Maclauin series for $~\sin x\,$, we obtain the given series.

Hence,

$\displaystyle-\sin x=-x+\frac{{{x}^{3}}}{3!}-\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{5!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+1}}}{\left( 2n+1 \right)!}+...$

assessment_items.question_content:
**$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}{{x}^{n}}}{n!}}~~$ is the Maclaurin series for which function?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First note that **all** possible factorials appear in the denominators. This suggests that the given series most closely resembles the Maclaurin series for the function $~{{e}^{x}}$.

We need to get the signs of the terms to alternate in sign.  Use $~-x~$ instead of $~x\,$.


$\displaystyle{{e}^{-x}}=1-x+\frac{{{x}^{2}}}{2!}-...+\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}{{x}^{n}}}{n!}+...~=~\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{\frac{{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n}}{{x}^{n}}}{n!}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**$\displaystyle\sum\limits_{n=0}^{\infty }{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+3}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}~~$ is the Maclaurin series for which function?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Note that the terms of the given series alternate in sign and have only **even** factorials in the denominator.  This suggests that we consider the Maclaurin series for the function $~\cos x\,$.

We need the sign of each term to be changed, and we need each of the powers of $~x~$ to be larger by 3.

Hence we have

$\displaystyle -x^3 \cos~ x=-x^3+\frac{{{x}^{5}}}{2!}-\frac{{{x}^{7}}}{4!}-...+{{\left( -1 \right)}^{n+1}}\frac{{{x}^{2n+3}}}{\left( 2n \right)!}+...\,$
